architectures.name: Proposed ATM Banking Software Architecture
architectures.description: A service-oriented architecture for ATM banking operations built using Fuzzy Association Rules (FAR) and Erlang distribution modeling. The system provides four core transactional services that operate on customer account balances. Each operation functions as a discrete service handling specific banking transactions through the ATM interface.
architectures.architecture_characteristics: Transaction integrity, reliability (Erlang distribution-based), rule-based decision making (FAR), operation-centric design. The architecture prioritizes correctness of financial transactions and appears designed for reliability analysis and fault tolerance assessment.

key_components.responsibilities: Retrieves and displays current account balance for authenticated users. Read-only operation that queries account data without modification.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Low - Simple query operation with minimal business logic

key_components.responsibilities: Core operation that modifies account balance values. Likely serves as a foundational service used by other transactional operations to persist balance changes.
key_components.change_sensitivity: High - Critical component affecting all balance-modifying operations; changes impact system integrity and data consistency

key_components.responsibilities: Processes deposit transactions by accepting funds and increasing account balance. Validates deposit amounts and updates account records.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - Business logic changes (deposit limits, validation rules) are moderately contained but affect customer-facing functionality

key_components.responsibilities: Processes withdrawal transactions by dispensing cash and decreasing account balance. Validates sufficient funds and withdrawal limits before processing.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - Business logic changes (withdrawal limits, overdraft rules) are moderately contained; has hardware integration dependencies

architectures.data_flow: 1) User initiates transaction at ATM → 2) Authentication occurs (implied) → 3) User selects operation (GetBalance, Deposit, or Withdraw) → 4) For GetBalance: C-01 queries account data and returns balance → 5) For Deposit: C-03 validates input, calls C-02 to update balance, confirms transaction → 6) For Withdraw: C-04 checks balance via C-01, validates withdrawal, calls C-02 to update balance, triggers cash dispensing → 7) Transaction completion and receipt generation. All balance modifications flow through the Update Balance component (C-02) ensuring centralized data consistency.
architectural_constraints: Operation-centric design lacks explicit presentation, business logic, and data access layers
No explicit error handling, rollback, or compensation mechanisms described
Missing authentication, authorization, and security components
No explicit transaction coordination or ACID compliance mechanisms specified
Fuzzy Association Rules (FAR) methodology not clearly mapped to component interactions
Erlang distribution application unclear - may relate to reliability/availability modeling rather than runtime behavior
No integration points defined for external systems (core banking, card networks, fraud detection)
Scalability approach undefined - appears designed for single-ATM deployment

easily_accommodated: Adding new transaction types (similar operation pattern)
Modifying individual operation business rules (deposit/withdrawal limits)
Reliability and availability analysis using Erlang distribution
Rule-based decision logic changes via FAR framework
difficult_to_accommodate: Multi-channel support (mobile, web) - architecture is ATM-specific
Complex transaction workflows (transfers between accounts, bill payments)
Integration with external banking systems and card networks
Distributed deployment across multiple ATMs with centralized coordination
Comprehensive audit trails and regulatory compliance reporting
Advanced security features (encryption, tokenization, multi-factor authentication)
Horizontal scaling for high-volume transaction processing

architectures.notes: This architecture appears to be a conceptual or analytical model rather than a complete implementation blueprint. The emphasis on Fuzzy Association Rules and Erlang distribution suggests this may be designed for academic research or reliability modeling rather than production deployment. Critical production concerns (security, persistence, network communication, hardware integration, regulatory compliance) are not addressed. Stakeholders should note that significant architectural elaboration would be required before this could serve as a production system design. The flat, operation-centric structure lacks the layering and separation of concerns typical of enterprise banking systems.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user inserts their card at 2:00 PM on a weekday, requests account balance, and receives the current balance displayed on screen within 3 seconds with 99.9% availability.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match - GetBalance component exists in current architecture. Performance and availability metrics need verification against Erlang distribution modeling capabilities.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Core functionality that customers depend on daily. Any failure directly impacts customer satisfaction and trust in the banking system.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user initiates a cash withdrawal of $200, the system verifies sufficient balance, dispenses cash, updates the account balance, and prints a receipt showing the transaction within 10 seconds.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match - all required components exist. Requires orchestration between three services. Receipt printing may be external hardware interface not explicitly modeled.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Primary ATM function. Transaction integrity and speed are critical for user experience and operational efficiency.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user deposits $500 in cash, the system counts and verifies the deposit, updates the account balance, and provides confirmation within 15 seconds with transaction logged for audit.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match - components exist. Physical cash counting mechanism and audit logging not explicitly shown in architecture. May require additional integration points.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Essential banking service. Accuracy and reliability are paramount as errors directly affect customer account balances.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer needs to add a new 'Transfer Between Accounts' operation that debits one account and credits another as an atomic transaction, completing within 5 seconds while maintaining ACID properties.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - requires new service component. Current architecture has Update Balance but lacks transaction coordination mechanism for multi-account operations. Service-oriented architecture should support adding new services, but atomic cross-account transactions may require distributed transaction management not currently specified.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Competitive feature requested by customers. Requires understanding of how to extend the current service architecture while maintaining data consistency.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer must integrate a new fraud detection service that intercepts all Withdraw and Deposit operations, analyzes transaction patterns using machine learning, and blocks suspicious transactions within 2 seconds without degrading normal operation performance.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - requires architectural modification to add interception points. Service-oriented architecture may support this through service chaining or middleware, but current schema-based design may not have explicit extension points. Fuzzy Association Rules might provide pattern matching foundation.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Regulatory compliance and risk management requirement. Developer needs clear extension mechanisms and performance impact assessment.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system maintainer needs to update the GetBalance service logic to include pending transactions in the displayed balance, deploy the change during a 2-hour maintenance window, and rollback within 15 minutes if errors occur.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - modifies existing component behavior. Service-oriented architecture should support independent service updates. Requires understanding of service versioning, deployment strategy, and rollback mechanisms not explicitly documented in current architecture.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Business requirement change that affects existing functionality. Maintainer needs clear deployment procedures and minimal system downtime.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer must diagnose why 0.5% of Withdraw operations are failing, trace transactions across GetBalance, Withdraw, and Update Balance components, identify the root cause within 30 minutes using system logs and monitoring tools.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - current architecture doesn't specify logging, monitoring, or tracing capabilities. Service-oriented architectures typically need observability infrastructure. Erlang distribution modeling might provide statistical analysis but not operational diagnostics.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Operational reliability requirement. System must be maintainable with adequate diagnostic capabilities to minimize downtime and customer impact.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator needs to scale the Withdraw service to handle 3x normal transaction volume during month-end peak periods (from 100 to 300 transactions per minute) without service degradation or downtime.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - architecture doesn't specify scalability mechanisms. Erlang distribution modeling may help predict load patterns, but actual scaling strategy (horizontal/vertical, load balancing, state management) is not defined. Service-oriented architecture should theoretically support scaling individual services.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Performance and availability requirement during predictable peak loads. Administrator needs infrastructure that supports elastic scaling.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator must configure automated failover so that if the primary Update Balance service fails, a secondary instance takes over within 30 seconds with zero transaction loss and automatic synchronization of account states.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - requires high availability architecture not currently specified. Needs redundancy, health monitoring, failover orchestration, and state replication mechanisms. Schema-based construction may not inherently support distributed state management.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Business continuity and regulatory requirement. Critical financial transactions cannot tolerate extended downtime or data loss.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator needs to apply security patches to the underlying operating system and restart all ATM services with total system downtime not exceeding 15 minutes during a scheduled 3 AM maintenance window.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - architecture doesn't specify deployment topology, service dependencies, or startup sequences. Service-oriented architecture should support independent service lifecycle, but coordination and startup time requirements are not documented.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Operational maintenance requirement. System must support routine maintenance with minimal business impact and predictable recovery times.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user attempts to withdraw an amount exceeding their account balance, and the system denies the transaction within 3 seconds with a clear error message explaining insufficient funds, without updating the account balance.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match - components exist but business logic validation is not explicitly documented. Requires GetBalance to check funds before Withdraw executes. Error handling and user messaging mechanisms not specified in architecture.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Critical business rule enforcement. Prevents overdrafts and ensures transaction integrity while providing clear user feedback.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer needs to modify the Deposit operation to support check deposits with image capture, requiring integration with optical character recognition (OCR) service and extending processing time to 30 seconds while maintaining the same Update Balance interface.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - requires significant extension of Deposit service with external integration. Service-oriented architecture should support service enhancement, but schema-based construction may require schema modifications. Impact on service contracts and backward compatibility unclear.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Competitive feature for customer convenience. Developer needs to understand extensibility patterns and integration capabilities of current architecture.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer must refactor the Update Balance component to use a new database schema while ensuring all dependent services (Withdraw, Deposit) continue functioning without modification during a phased migration over 2 weeks.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - requires understanding of service coupling and data abstraction. Service-oriented architecture should provide loose coupling, but schema file construction suggests tight schema dependency. Migration strategy and backward compatibility mechanisms not specified.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Technical debt reduction or technology upgrade. Maintainer needs architecture that supports evolution without requiring simultaneous changes across all services.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator needs to implement geographic redundancy by deploying the ATM system across two data centers 500 miles apart, with automatic failover and account balance synchronization latency under 100 milliseconds.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect - requires distributed systems architecture not currently specified. Geographic distribution introduces network latency, data consistency challenges (CAP theorem), and complex failover logic. Current architecture doesn't address distributed state management or replication strategies.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Disaster recovery and business continuity planning. Administrator needs to understand if architecture can support geographic distribution without fundamental redesign.

scenario_classification.rationale: While GetBalance component exists and provides the core functionality, the architecture lacks explicit specifications for: (1) performance monitoring to guarantee 3-second response time, (2) availability infrastructure to ensure 99.9% uptime, (3) authentication mechanisms for card insertion, and (4) ATM display interface integration. The Erlang distribution modeling mentioned may support availability analysis but doesn't guarantee runtime availability.

Core components (GetBalance, Withdraw, Update Balance) exist and their orchestration pattern is documented in data flow. However, the architecture lacks: (1) explicit transaction orchestration to ensure atomic execution across three services, (2) cash dispenser hardware interface specification, (3) receipt printing integration, (4) performance guarantees for 10-second completion, and (5) rollback mechanisms if cash dispensing fails after balance update.

Deposit and Update Balance components exist, but the architecture lacks: (1) physical cash counting hardware interface, (2) explicit audit logging mechanism (only implied in constraints), (3) transaction verification workflow, (4) performance monitoring for 15-second guarantee, and (5) error handling for cash counting discrepancies. The audit requirement is particularly critical for regulatory compliance but not architecturally specified.

While the service-oriented architecture theoretically supports adding new services, the architecture explicitly lacks: (1) transaction coordination mechanisms for atomic multi-account operations, (2) distributed transaction management (2PC, SAGA patterns), (3) ACID compliance infrastructure, and (4) cross-account locking mechanisms. The current Update Balance component operates on single accounts. This is listed in 'difficult_to_accommodate' scenarios as 'Complex transaction workflows'.

The architecture lacks: (1) explicit service interception or middleware extension points, (2) service chaining mechanisms, (3) integration framework for external services, (4) performance monitoring to ensure 2-second analysis doesn't degrade operations, and (5) transaction blocking/rejection workflow. While FAR (Fuzzy Association Rules) might provide pattern matching foundation, integration architecture is not specified. The flat operation-centric design doesn't show clear extension points.

While modifying GetBalance business logic is listed as 'easily_accommodated', the scenario requires: (1) service versioning strategy not specified in architecture, (2) deployment infrastructure and procedures, (3) rollback mechanisms explicitly noted as missing in constraints, (4) pending transactions data source integration, and (5) maintenance window coordination. The architecture describes components but not their deployment or lifecycle management.

The architecture explicitly lacks observability infrastructure. Missing capabilities include: (1) logging framework across services, (2) distributed tracing for cross-component transaction flows, (3) monitoring tools and dashboards, (4) correlation IDs for transaction tracking, and (5) diagnostic interfaces. The Erlang distribution modeling provides statistical analysis but not operational diagnostics. This is a critical gap for production systems.

The architecture explicitly states 'Scalability approach undefined - appears designed for single-ATM deployment' and lists 'Horizontal scaling for high-volume transaction processing' as difficult to accommodate. Missing elements include: (1) load balancing mechanisms, (2) horizontal scaling infrastructure, (3) stateless service design verification, (4) shared state management for scaled instances, and (5) elastic scaling policies. Erlang distribution may predict load but doesn't enable scaling.

High availability architecture is not specified. The scenario requires: (1) service redundancy and replication, (2) health monitoring and failure detection, (3) automated failover orchestration, (4) state synchronization between primary and secondary instances, (5) zero-loss transaction queuing, and (6) distributed state management. Update Balance is marked as 'High' change sensitivity, making HA particularly critical but architecturally unsupported.

The architecture lacks: (1) deployment topology specification, (2) service dependency graph and startup sequencing, (3) graceful shutdown procedures, (4) service lifecycle management, (5) startup time characteristics, and (6) health check mechanisms. While service-oriented architecture should support independent service lifecycle, the coordination required for 15-minute total downtime is not architecturally defined.

The data flow shows Withdraw checks balance via GetBalance, suggesting validation capability exists. However, the architecture lacks: (1) explicit business rule validation framework, (2) error handling mechanisms (noted as missing in constraints), (3) user messaging/notification system, (4) validation rule configuration, and (5) performance guarantees for 3-second denial. The validation logic is implied but not architecturally specified.

The architecture lacks: (1) external service integration framework, (2) asynchronous processing capabilities for 30-second operations, (3) hardware integration for image capture, (4) service contract versioning to maintain Update Balance interface compatibility, and (5) backward compatibility mechanisms. The schema-based construction may require schema modifications. This represents significant service enhancement beyond current scope.

The architecture notes 'schema file construction suggests tight schema dependency' which directly conflicts with this scenario. Missing elements include: (1) data abstraction layer to decouple services from schema, (2) adapter pattern or anti-corruption layer, (3) dual-write or phased migration strategy, (4) backward compatibility mechanisms, and (5) service contract stability guarantees. The schema-based construction approach makes this particularly challenging.

The architecture explicitly states 'Distributed deployment across multiple ATMs with centralized coordination' is difficult to accommodate. Geographic redundancy requires: (1) distributed systems architecture, (2) data replication strategy (synchronous/asynchronous), (3) CAP theorem trade-off decisions, (4) network partition handling, (5) conflict resolution for concurrent updates, (6) geographic failover orchestration, and (7) sub-100ms synchronization infrastructure. This represents fundamental architectural redesign.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Add authentication service for card validation, implement performance monitoring infrastructure to track response times, add availability monitoring aligned with Erlang distribution modeling, create ATM display interface abstraction, and implement SLA enforcement mechanisms for 3-second response and 99.9% availability targets.
issues_identified: No authentication mechanism specified in current architecture
Performance SLA enforcement requires monitoring infrastructure not present
Availability guarantee requires HA infrastructure beyond Erlang modeling
ATM hardware interface layer missing
No explicit performance budgeting across service calls
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement GetBalance with embedded performance tracking and timeout mechanisms. Use Erlang distribution modeling to predict and provision for 99.9% availability. Add simple authentication wrapper around GetBalance service. Accept that full SLA enforcement may require external monitoring tools initially.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement transaction coordinator to orchestrate GetBalance → Withdraw → Update Balance sequence atomically. Add compensating transaction logic for rollback if cash dispensing fails. Create hardware abstraction layer for cash dispenser integration. Implement receipt generation service. Add distributed transaction support or SAGA pattern for atomicity across services.
issues_identified: No transaction coordination mechanism for multi-service operations
Missing rollback/compensation logic if hardware fails after balance update
Cash dispenser hardware interface not architecturally defined
Receipt printing integration missing
10-second performance budget requires careful service orchestration
Potential for inconsistent state if Update Balance succeeds but cash dispensing fails
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement two-phase commit pattern where Update Balance is only called after successful cash dispensing (reverse order). Use optimistic locking on account balance. Implement idempotency tokens to allow safe retries. Consider making Withdraw service the coordinator that internally calls GetBalance and Update Balance to reduce distributed transaction complexity.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Add hardware interface for cash counting/verification. Implement comprehensive audit logging service with tamper-proof transaction records. Create verification workflow to handle cash counting discrepancies. Add performance monitoring for 15-second guarantee. Implement transaction reconciliation mechanisms between physical cash and digital records.
issues_identified: Physical cash counting hardware interface not specified
Audit logging noted as missing in architectural constraints
No reconciliation process for cash count vs. user-claimed amount
Regulatory compliance requirements for audit trails not addressed
Error handling for hardware failures during deposit not defined
Risk of balance update without confirmed cash receipt
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement Deposit service with embedded audit logging that writes to immutable transaction log before calling Update Balance. Use event sourcing pattern where all deposit events are logged and balance is derived. Implement two-phase deposit: provisional credit pending cash verification, then final confirmation. Add manual reconciliation workflow for discrepancies.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Create new Transfer service component. Implement distributed transaction manager supporting two-phase commit or SAGA pattern for atomic multi-account operations. Add account locking mechanism to prevent concurrent modifications. Extend Update Balance to support transaction context and rollback. Implement compensation logic for partial failures. Add cross-account validation and authorization.
issues_identified: No distributed transaction coordination infrastructure
Update Balance designed for single-account operations
ACID compliance mechanisms explicitly missing from architecture
Cross-account locking strategy undefined - risk of deadlocks
Atomicity requirement conflicts with service-oriented loose coupling
Listed as 'difficult to accommodate' in architecture assessment
Potential for inconsistent state if debit succeeds but credit fails
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement SAGA pattern with compensating transactions: debit source account, if successful credit destination account, if credit fails compensate by re-crediting source. Use event sourcing where transfer is recorded as single event affecting both accounts. Implement Transfer as orchestrator service that manages the workflow. Consider eventual consistency model with reconciliation rather than strict ACID. Use optimistic locking with version numbers on account balances.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement service interceptor or middleware layer to inject fraud detection before Withdraw/Deposit execution. Create fraud detection service with ML integration. Add service chaining or pipeline pattern to architecture. Implement circuit breaker to prevent fraud service failures from blocking transactions. Add asynchronous analysis option if 2-second synchronous analysis is too slow. Extend FAR framework to incorporate ML-based pattern matching.
issues_identified: No service interception or middleware extension points defined
Flat operation-centric design lacks clear extension mechanisms
2-second analysis requirement may conflict with ML processing time
Integration with external ML service not architecturally supported
Risk of fraud service becoming single point of failure
Performance degradation risk if fraud analysis is synchronous
Transaction blocking workflow not defined
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Leverage Fuzzy Association Rules (FAR) framework mentioned in architecture for rule-based fraud detection instead of ML initially. Implement fraud detection as decorator pattern around Withdraw/Deposit services. Use asynchronous fraud analysis with post-transaction review and reversal capability. Implement fraud scoring that flags suspicious transactions for manual review rather than automatic blocking. Add fraud detection as optional plugin that can be enabled/disabled per deployment.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement service versioning strategy (semantic versioning, API versioning). Create deployment automation with blue-green or canary deployment capability. Add rollback mechanisms with automated health checks. Integrate pending transactions data source with GetBalance. Implement feature flags for gradual rollout. Add deployment orchestration for maintenance window management.
issues_identified: Service versioning strategy not specified
Deployment procedures and infrastructure undefined
Rollback mechanisms explicitly noted as missing
Pending transactions data source not part of current architecture
Maintenance window coordination not addressed
No specification of service dependencies that might break during update
15-minute rollback requirement needs automated health validation
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement GetBalance v2 alongside v1 with routing based on feature flag. Use database view or query modification to include pending transactions without service logic change. Deploy new version to subset of ATMs first for validation. Implement automated smoke tests that trigger rollback if failures detected. Use immutable deployment artifacts that can be quickly swapped. Maintain backward compatibility by making pending transactions optional in response.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement structured logging framework across all services with consistent log formats. Add distributed tracing using correlation IDs to track transactions across GetBalance → Withdraw → Update Balance flow. Create centralized log aggregation and search capability. Implement monitoring dashboards with transaction success/failure metrics. Add diagnostic endpoints to each service. Implement transaction replay capability for debugging.
issues_identified: Logging and monitoring infrastructure explicitly missing from architecture
No distributed tracing for cross-component transaction flows
Erlang distribution provides statistical modeling but not operational diagnostics
30-minute diagnosis requirement needs comprehensive observability
Root cause analysis requires correlation across three services
No specification of what data should be logged for diagnostics
Critical gap for production operations and maintenance
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement lightweight logging initially with transaction IDs passed through service calls. Use existing Erlang distribution modeling to identify statistical anomalies. Add debug mode that enables verbose logging on-demand. Implement service health endpoints that expose internal state. Use database transaction logs as primary diagnostic source. Create transaction timeline reconstruction from database audit trail. Implement sampling-based tracing to reduce overhead.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Verify Withdraw service is stateless or externalize state. Implement load balancing across multiple Withdraw service instances. Add service registry for dynamic instance discovery. Implement auto-scaling policies based on transaction volume metrics. Add shared cache or database for any shared state. Implement connection pooling for database access. Use Erlang distribution modeling to predict scaling needs and trigger proactive scaling.
issues_identified: Scalability approach explicitly undefined in architecture
Architecture appears designed for single-ATM deployment
Horizontal scaling listed as 'difficult to accommodate'
Stateless service design not verified
No load balancing mechanism specified
Shared state management strategy undefined
3x scaling requirement (100→300 TPS) needs infrastructure not present
Cash dispenser hardware integration may complicate scaling
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use Erlang distribution modeling to predict peak loads and pre-provision capacity. Implement vertical scaling (larger instances) instead of horizontal scaling initially. Use queue-based load leveling to smooth peak loads. Implement request throttling and queuing at ATM level. Cache account balances to reduce database load. Optimize Withdraw service performance to handle higher throughput on existing infrastructure. Consider that physical ATM hardware may be the bottleneck, not software.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement active-passive or active-active redundancy for Update Balance service. Add health monitoring with heartbeat and failure detection (sub-30 second detection). Implement automated failover orchestration. Add synchronous or asynchronous state replication between primary and secondary instances. Implement transaction queuing with guaranteed delivery to prevent transaction loss. Add distributed consensus mechanism (Raft, Paxos) for state consistency.
issues_identified: High availability architecture not specified
Update Balance marked as 'High' change sensitivity - critical component
State synchronization mechanism undefined
Zero transaction loss requires durable queuing not present
30-second failover requires automated orchestration
Distributed state management not addressed in architecture
Risk of split-brain scenarios without consensus mechanism
Database-level HA may be required in addition to service-level HA
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement database-level high availability (database replication, clustering) rather than service-level HA. Use synchronous database replication to ensure zero data loss. Implement Update Balance as thin wrapper around database, relying on database HA. Use message queue with persistence for all balance updates. Implement idempotent Update Balance operations to allow safe retries. Use active-passive failover with shared database to simplify state management. Leverage Erlang distribution modeling to calculate required redundancy levels.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Document service dependency graph and startup sequence. Implement graceful shutdown procedures for each service. Add health check endpoints to verify service readiness. Create service orchestration for coordinated startup/shutdown. Implement connection draining to complete in-flight transactions. Add startup time optimization and parallel initialization where possible. Create deployment automation for maintenance windows.
issues_identified: Deployment topology not specified
Service dependency graph undefined
Startup sequencing not documented
Graceful shutdown procedures missing
15-minute downtime budget requires optimized restart procedures
Health check mechanisms not defined
Risk of failed startup if service dependencies not properly sequenced
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement simple startup script with fixed sequence: Database → Update Balance → GetBalance/Deposit/Withdraw in parallel. Add timeout-based health checks (service responds within 5 seconds = healthy). Implement pre-warming of caches during startup. Use rolling restart if multiple instances exist. Document startup time for each service through testing. Implement startup health dashboard visible to administrators. Practice maintenance procedures in test environment to validate 15-minute target.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement business rule validation framework within Withdraw service. Add explicit error handling and exception management (noted as missing). Create user messaging service for error notifications. Implement validation rule configuration (withdrawal limits, overdraft rules). Add performance monitoring to ensure 3-second response for denials. Ensure GetBalance is called before withdrawal attempt and result is used for validation.
issues_identified: Business rule validation framework not explicitly specified
Error handling mechanisms noted as missing in constraints
User messaging/notification system not defined
Validation logic implied in data flow but not architecturally detailed
Performance guarantee for error cases not specified
Risk of inconsistent validation logic across different transaction types
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement validation logic directly in Withdraw service using GetBalance result. Use Fuzzy Association Rules (FAR) framework for configurable business rules. Return structured error codes that ATM interface translates to user messages. Implement validation as pre-condition check before any balance modification. Use guard clauses in Withdraw service to fail fast on validation errors. Cache recent balance checks to avoid redundant GetBalance calls for validation.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Create Deposit v2 service with check deposit capability. Implement asynchronous processing framework for 30-second operations. Add hardware interface for check image capture. Integrate external OCR service. Implement provisional credit workflow (immediate provisional credit, final credit after verification). Maintain Update Balance interface compatibility through adapter pattern. Add service versioning to support both cash and check deposits.
issues_identified: External service integration framework not specified
Asynchronous processing capability not present (current operations appear synchronous)
Hardware integration for image capture not defined
30-second processing time conflicts with current synchronous design
Service contract versioning strategy undefined
Schema-based construction may require schema modifications
Backward compatibility with existing Deposit callers at risk
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement check deposit as separate service (CheckDeposit) rather than extending Deposit, avoiding backward compatibility issues. Use two-phase deposit: capture image and provide provisional credit immediately, then asynchronous OCR processing with final settlement. Implement callback or polling mechanism for asynchronous completion. Use message queue for OCR processing to decouple from synchronous flow. Maintain simple Deposit service for cash, add CheckDeposit service for checks, both using Update Balance. Implement feature flag to enable check deposit capability per ATM.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement data access abstraction layer between services and database. Create adapter pattern to support both old and new schemas simultaneously. Implement dual-write strategy during migration (write to both schemas). Add schema version detection in Update Balance. Create migration orchestrator for phased rollout. Implement backward-compatible service contracts. Add data synchronization and validation between schemas.
issues_identified: Schema-based construction suggests tight coupling to database schema
No data abstraction layer present
Service contract stability not guaranteed
Phased migration strategy not supported by current architecture
Risk of breaking dependent services during schema change
2-week migration window requires sophisticated dual-schema support
Data consistency between old and new schemas during migration
Rollback strategy if migration fails mid-process
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement database views that present new schema as old schema to existing services. Use database-level schema migration with backward-compatible changes only (additive changes). Implement Update Balance v2 that supports new schema while v1 continues with old schema. Use strangler pattern: gradually migrate services to new Update Balance v2. Implement data synchronization triggers at database level. Consider big-bang migration during maintenance window instead of phased approach. Use feature flags to control which schema version each service uses.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement distributed database with geographic replication (synchronous for <100ms latency). Add geographic load balancing and failover orchestration. Implement conflict resolution for concurrent updates across data centers. Add network partition detection and handling. Implement distributed consensus mechanism. Add CAP theorem trade-off decisions (likely CP for financial transactions). Implement cross-datacenter transaction coordination. This represents fundamental architectural redesign from single-deployment to distributed system.
issues_identified: Architecture explicitly designed for single-ATM deployment
Distributed deployment listed as 'difficult to accommodate'
No distributed systems architecture present
100ms synchronization latency extremely challenging over 500 miles
CAP theorem trade-offs not addressed
Network partition handling not specified
Conflict resolution for concurrent updates undefined
Geographic failover orchestration not present
This scenario requires fundamental architectural redesign
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use asynchronous replication with eventual consistency instead of 100ms synchronous replication (relax latency requirement). Implement active-passive geographic redundancy where secondary datacenter is read-only backup. Use database-level geographic replication (e.g., distributed SQL database). Partition data by geography (customers assigned to primary datacenter) to reduce cross-datacenter transactions. Implement CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) for account balances. Accept higher latency (seconds) for cross-datacenter synchronization. Use Erlang distribution modeling to determine if geographic redundancy is actually needed based on failure probability analysis.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: Update Balance is the most critical hotspot (9 scenarios). It's central to ACID compliance (S-04), transaction atomicity (S-02), high availability with zero data loss (S-09), and schema migration (S-13). Failures here directly cause financial inconsistencies, transaction loss, and regulatory violations. All stakeholders impacted: end-users experience failed transactions, developers face distributed transaction complexity, maintainers struggle with schema changes, and administrators must ensure 30-second failover. The component is marked 'High' change sensitivity and lacks distributed state management, HA infrastructure, and data abstraction - all critical gaps for a financial system.

Withdraw service is critical for core ATM functionality (8 scenarios). It orchestrates complex multi-service transactions (S-02) requiring transaction coordination, hardware integration, and compensation logic. Scaling requirements (S-08: 100→300 TPS) are unaddressed with horizontal scaling marked 'difficult to accommodate'. Fraud detection integration (S-05) requires middleware/interception capabilities not present. Business rule validation (S-11) lacks error handling framework. End-users face failed withdrawals and fraud exposure, developers face high-effort distributed transaction implementation, administrators struggle with scaling and monitoring. Missing transaction coordinator and service interception points create high operational risk.

GetBalance is foundational for all transaction workflows (8 scenarios). Performance SLA enforcement (S-01: 3-second response, 99.9% availability) requires monitoring infrastructure not present. Diagnostic capabilities (S-07: 30-minute diagnosis) are critically missing with no distributed tracing or logging framework. Deployment/rollback mechanisms (S-06) are undefined, risking extended outages. Service versioning strategy absent for pending transactions integration. End-users experience poor performance and availability, developers lack observability for debugging, maintainers face risky deployments without rollback, administrators cannot diagnose production issues. The 'widespread' logging/monitoring gap affects operational viability across all scenarios.

Deposit service has significant but more contained risks (6 scenarios). Audit logging (S-03) is explicitly missing, creating regulatory compliance exposure and reconciliation challenges - high risk but localized to deposit operations. Check deposit capability (S-12) requires asynchronous processing framework not present, but is a feature extension rather than core functionality fix. Fraud detection integration (S-05) shares middleware gaps with Withdraw. End-users face deposit reconciliation issues and delayed check processing, developers must implement async processing and OCR integration (high effort), maintainers deal with schema versioning for new capabilities. While audit logging is critical for compliance, the component affects fewer core transaction flows than C-02/C-04/C-01, justifying Medium priority despite high-risk scenarios.

architectural_strengths: Strength 1: Clean service decomposition for core banking operations - The architecture successfully decomposes ATM functionality into four focused services (GetBalance, UpdateBalance, Withdraw, Deposit) with clear responsibilities. This enables straightforward implementation of basic scenarios like balance inquiries (S-01) and simple business rule validation (S-11) with localized changes and low effort.

Strength 2: Fuzzy Association Rules (FAR) framework provides extensibility foundation - The existing FAR framework offers a configurable rule-based mechanism that can support fraud detection (S-05) and business rule validation (S-11) without requiring immediate ML integration. This provides a lightweight alternative approach for multiple scenarios.

Strength 3: Erlang distribution modeling for statistical analysis - The architecture incorporates Erlang distribution modeling for performance and availability prediction, providing analytical foundation for capacity planning (S-08), SLA enforcement (S-01), and availability guarantees. This mathematical rigor supports data-driven infrastructure decisions.

Strength 4: Schema-based construction enables rapid service generation - The schema-driven approach allows quick creation of new service variants and supports the four core operations efficiently. This strength is evident in the ability to conceptually extend services like Deposit for new capabilities (S-12), though it creates coupling challenges addressed in weaknesses.
architectural_weaknesses: Weakness 1: Missing distributed transaction coordination infrastructure - Critical gap affecting 5 high-risk scenarios (S-02, S-04, S-09, S-13, S-14). The architecture lacks transaction coordinators, SAGA patterns, two-phase commit, or distributed consensus mechanisms required for atomic multi-service operations. This creates severe risk of financial inconsistencies, particularly for withdrawals (S-02: balance update succeeds but cash dispensing fails) and transfers (S-04: explicitly marked 'difficult to accommodate'). Impact: High-effort implementations required, potential for data loss and regulatory violations.

Weakness 2: Absent operational infrastructure (logging, monitoring, observability) - Explicitly noted as missing and affecting 8 scenarios (S-01, S-02, S-07, S-08, S-09, S-10, S-11, S-14). No distributed tracing, structured logging, health checks, or monitoring dashboards exist. This cripples production operations: 30-minute diagnosis requirement (S-07) is impossible without observability, SLA enforcement (S-01) cannot be validated, and failover detection (S-09: 30-second requirement) lacks health monitoring. Impact: System is operationally unviable for production deployment.

Weakness 3: No high availability or resilience architecture - Affects 4 high-risk scenarios (S-01, S-08, S-09, S-14). Architecture designed for single-ATM deployment with horizontal scaling marked 'difficult to accommodate' (S-08). Missing: load balancers, service registry, state replication, failover orchestration, auto-scaling. The 99.9% availability target (S-01) and zero transaction loss requirement (S-09) are architecturally unsupported. Geographic distribution (S-14) requires complete redesign. Impact: Cannot meet availability SLAs or scale beyond single deployment.

Weakness 4: Tight schema coupling without data abstraction layer - Affects 3 scenarios including high-risk schema migration (S-13). Schema-based construction creates direct coupling between services and database schema, making phased migrations extremely difficult (S-13: 2-week migration window unsupported). No adapter pattern, data access abstraction, or service contract versioning exists. This also complicates service evolution (S-06, S-12) and backward compatibility. Impact: High-risk deployments, brittle service contracts, difficult schema evolution.

Weakness 5: Missing hardware integration and external service frameworks - Affects 6 scenarios (S-02, S-03, S-05, S-08, S-12, S-14). No architectural support for: cash dispenser/acceptor interfaces (S-02, S-03), OCR integration (S-12), ML service integration (S-05), or asynchronous processing (S-12: 30-second operations). The flat operation-centric design lacks extension points, middleware layers, or service interception capabilities. Impact: Each hardware/external integration requires widespread architectural changes.

Weakness 6: Inadequate error handling and audit mechanisms - Explicitly noted as missing and affecting regulatory compliance scenarios (S-03, S-11). No tamper-proof audit logging for deposits (S-03: regulatory requirement), no structured error handling framework (S-11), and no reconciliation processes for physical-digital discrepancies (S-03: cash count vs. claimed amount). Impact: Regulatory compliance violations, poor user experience, operational reconciliation challenges.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture exhibits poor modifiability with 13 of 14 scenarios classified as 'Indirect' requiring widespread changes and medium-to-high effort. While core service decomposition is clean, the absence of critical cross-cutting infrastructure (transaction coordination, observability, HA, data abstraction, hardware integration frameworks) means virtually every realistic scenario requires architectural additions rather than configuration or localized changes. The architecture is operationally immature and structurally rigid for evolution beyond basic single-ATM deployment.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement comprehensive observability infrastructure (distributed tracing, structured logging, health checks, monitoring dashboards) across all services with correlation IDs for transaction flows
recommendations.rationale: Addresses the most widespread gap affecting 8 scenarios and all stakeholder roles. Without observability, the system cannot be diagnosed (S-07), monitored for SLAs (S-01), or operated in production (S-09, S-10). This is a foundational capability required before addressing other weaknesses. Enables developers to debug distributed transactions, administrators to detect failures, and maintainers to validate deployments. Quick win: Start with lightweight transaction ID propagation and structured logging before full distributed tracing.

recommendations.recommendation: Introduce data abstraction layer (Repository/DAO pattern) between services and database schema, with adapter pattern support for schema versioning
recommendations.rationale: Breaks critical schema coupling affecting Update Balance (C-02: highest priority hotspot with 9 scenarios). Enables phased schema migration (S-13), service contract stability (S-06, S-12), and reduces ripple effects from database changes. This architectural pattern improves modifiability across all data-dependent scenarios and reduces risk for the most change-sensitive component. Supports both backward compatibility and independent service evolution.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement SAGA pattern with compensating transactions for multi-service operations (Withdraw, Transfer) rather than distributed two-phase commit
recommendations.rationale: Addresses distributed transaction coordination gap (Weakness 1) affecting critical financial operations (S-02, S-04) while maintaining service autonomy and avoiding complex distributed locking. SAGA pattern aligns with service-oriented architecture, provides better failure handling than 2PC, and enables eventual consistency acceptable for ATM operations. Specifically solves withdrawal atomicity (GetBalance → Withdraw → UpdateBalance) and enables future transfer capability. Alternative approaches in evaluations consistently recommend SAGA over 2PC for this architecture.

recommendations.recommendation: Establish hardware abstraction layer (HAL) with standardized interfaces for cash dispenser, cash acceptor, card reader, and receipt printer integration
recommendations.rationale: Creates extension points for hardware integration affecting 4 scenarios (S-02, S-03, S-08, S-12) and enables service interception capabilities needed for fraud detection (S-05). HAL pattern decouples business logic from hardware specifics, enables testing without physical devices, and provides consistent error handling for hardware failures. This addresses the 'flat operation-centric design lacks extension points' weakness and improves modifiability for future hardware changes.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement database-level high availability (synchronous replication, clustering) with health monitoring and automated failover orchestration for Update Balance service
recommendations.rationale: Addresses zero transaction loss requirement (S-09) and availability SLA (S-01) for the highest-priority component (C-02). Database-level HA is more feasible than service-level distributed state management given current architecture maturity. Synchronous replication ensures data durability, automated failover meets 30-second requirement, and this approach leverages proven database HA solutions rather than building custom distributed systems. Enables Update Balance to remain thin wrapper around database while achieving resilience.

recommendations.recommendation: Add tamper-proof audit logging service with immutable transaction records and event sourcing pattern for all financial operations
recommendations.rationale: Addresses regulatory compliance gap (S-03) and provides foundation for reconciliation, fraud detection (S-05), and diagnostics (S-07). Event sourcing pattern where all deposit/withdrawal/transfer events are logged before balance updates provides both audit trail and ability to reconstruct account state. This supports multiple scenarios while introducing architectural pattern that improves traceability and debugging. Critical for production deployment in regulated financial environment.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement service versioning strategy (semantic versioning, API versioning headers) with blue-green deployment capability and automated rollback based on health checks
recommendations.rationale: Addresses deployment and rollback gaps (S-06, S-10, S-12) affecting all services. Enables safe service evolution (GetBalance v2 with pending transactions, Deposit v2 with check capability) while maintaining backward compatibility. Blue-green deployment with automated health validation supports 15-minute rollback requirement and reduces deployment risk. This improves modifiability by allowing parallel versions during transitions and provides safety net for changes.

recommendations.recommendation: Introduce service mesh or API gateway pattern to provide cross-cutting capabilities (authentication, fraud detection interception, rate limiting, circuit breakers) without modifying individual services
recommendations.rationale: Addresses missing middleware/interception points (S-05) and provides infrastructure for authentication (S-01), performance monitoring, and resilience patterns. Service mesh enables fraud detection to be injected into request path without changing Withdraw/Deposit services, supports circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures, and provides consistent security/monitoring across services. This architectural pattern significantly improves extensibility and modifiability for cross-cutting concerns.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement vertical scaling optimization and caching strategy before horizontal scaling infrastructure, using Erlang distribution modeling to predict capacity needs
recommendations.rationale: Addresses scaling requirement (S-08: 100→300 TPS) pragmatically given 'horizontal scaling difficult to accommodate' assessment. Vertical scaling (larger instances), database connection pooling, and balance caching can achieve 3x throughput increase with lower complexity than distributed architecture. Erlang modeling can predict when vertical scaling limits are reached. This is realistic quick win before investing in service registry, load balancing, and stateless refactoring required for horizontal scaling.

recommendations.recommendation: Document service dependency graph, startup sequences, and create deployment runbooks with health check procedures for operational readiness
recommendations.rationale: Addresses deployment topology and graceful shutdown gaps (S-10) with low effort. While not solving architectural weaknesses, this documentation is essential for operators and reduces risk of failed deployments. Supports 15-minute maintenance window requirement through practiced procedures. Quick win that improves operational maturity and provides foundation for automated orchestration later.

recommendations.recommendation: Defer geographic distribution (S-14) and complex distributed systems architecture until core operational infrastructure (observability, HA, transaction coordination) is mature and business case is validated
recommendations.rationale: S-14 requires fundamental architectural redesign and conflicts with current single-deployment design. The 100ms synchronous replication over 500 miles is extremely challenging and may not be necessary - Erlang distribution modeling should determine if geographic redundancy is actually required based on failure probability analysis. Focus limited resources on foundational gaps (High priority recommendations) that affect current deployment viability before attempting distributed systems complexity.
